content:
Ryan, that’s a fair and pastoral question.

A couple of anchors first:
- God is indeed big enough to be honored by people who are honestly trying to read his Word and obey it, even when they land in different places on secondary matters.
- At the same time, we don’t edit Scripture to win respectability. The call to follow Christ includes social cost (1 Cor 1:18; Matt 5:11–12). But we also don’t add needless offense by insisting on what Scripture doesn’t actually require (Rom 14; Titus 3:9).

On “literal”: in the Christian tradition the “literal sense” means the author’s intended sense, taking genre and language conventions seriously—not a wooden, modern-scientific reading. Scripture often speaks phenomenologically: “sunrise,” “ends of the earth,” etc. That’s not error; it’s normal human speech accommodated to us. The Chicago Statement on Biblical Inerrancy explicitly affirms this.

About the firmament (raqia):
- The word can mean “expanse.” In Genesis 1 God calls the raqia “heavens,” and birds fly across it (Gen 1:20), which naturally reads as the sky. The “waters above” can be understood as the waters we see above us (clouds/precipitation) or as part of a grand, temple-like cosmic picture. Poetry can picture the sky as a hard surface (Job 37:18), but poetry trades in vivid images.
- Faithful, inerrantist readers have held different views: young-earth, old-earth, framework/analogical days, cosmic-temple readings. None of these require you to deny that Scripture is true. They differ on what, exactly, Genesis is intending to teach at the level of cosmology.

So yes: God can be honored either way—by the believer who says, “I think Genesis is using ancient-yet-true, phenomenological language to exalt God as Creator,” and by the believer who says, “I think Genesis describes a structure I can’t fully square with modern models, and I’m willing to bear that.” What matters is humble submission to what God actually meant to say, not to what will cost us least socially.

Two guardrails help keep us out of the ditch:
- Don’t make a secondary question a boundary of the faith. The gospel is of first importance (1 Cor 15:1–4). Unity in essentials, liberty in non-essentials, charity in all things.
- Don’t surrender convictions just to avoid embarrassment. If the text really compels you, bear the cost with a gentle, non-combative spirit (1 Pet 3:15; 2 Tim 2:24–25).

If you’re worried about “making Christianity unnecessarily hard,” remember: the cross is already a stumbling block. We should be careful not to add extra stumbling blocks of our own making, but we must not remove the ones God put there. The wise path is to major on what Scripture majors on and hold the rest with open-handed humility.

Practically, here are two faithful ways to speak about it:
- If you lean non-concordist: “I take Genesis as true in what it intends—God made and ordered everything. I don’t think the chapter is trying to give a modern scientific model, so I’m comfortable reading raqia as the sky/expanse in phenomenological terms.”
- If you lean strict literalist: “I trust Scripture’s description even when it puts me out of step. I don’t make this a test of fellowship, but I’m not embarrassed to stand with God’s Word.”

Either way, model careful reading, charity toward other Christians, and courage before a watching world. Ask the Lord for wisdom (James 1:5), stay tethered to the church’s core confession, and let the necessary offense be the beauty and lordship of Jesus—not our pet interpretations.

Ryan, I hear the tension you’re feeling. A few thoughts, as a brother who loves Scripture and wants to honor the Lord.

- Inerrancy isn’t the same thing as wooden literalism. The Bible is true in all it affirms, but it speaks in many genres and often with everyday, observational language. “Sunrise,” for example, is perfectly true speech even if it’s not technical astronomy. The Chicago Statement on Biblical Inerrancy actually says this kind of ordinary language doesn’t jeopardize truth.

- Genesis 1’s “firmament” (raqia) can be read faithfully more than one way. Many solid, Bible-honoring scholars understand it as the sky/expanse—God carving out ordered space—using the language and imagery familiar to ancient hearers. Others think it reflects ancient cosmology without intending to teach a scientific model. Either way, the text’s purpose is theological: Who made everything, why it’s ordered and good, and where humans fit under God’s rule. You don’t have to adopt a “solid dome” to take the passage seriously and submit to its authority.

- We should never trim truth to avoid social cost. Jesus told us there would be some. But we also shouldn’t create unnecessary stumbling blocks by binding consciences to an interpretation the text itself doesn’t demand. Augustine warned Christians not to speak carelessly about nature in a way that brings Scripture into needless disrepute. That’s not compromise; that’s humility about what a passage is actually trying to do.

- On “Isn’t God big enough to be honored either way?” Yes—if our posture is faith and submission to Scripture as God’s Word, and if we’re doing our best to read what the author intended. Faithful believers who hold inerrancy land in different places on the firmament because they weigh the genre, context, and language differently. That’s a Romans 14 kind of space: be fully convinced in your own mind, hold your view unto the Lord, and show charity to those who differ.

- With your kids, you can model both courage and humility: “We don’t change what God says just to fit in. At the same time, we don’t insist the Bible is answering scientific questions it wasn’t written to answer. We follow Jesus, tell the truth, and stay teachable.”

If your conscience before God leads you to a literal-dome reading, bear the cost with gentleness. If, after careful study, you’re persuaded the raqia is phenomenological or temple-cosmic imagery, you can hold that with a clear inerrantist conscience too. Let’s keep the main thing the main thing: the Creator, His image-bearers, the goodness of His world, Sabbath rest under His reign—and Christ, through whom and for whom all things were made.

*I turn the mug in my hands, tea gone cool, and watch a thin band of pale fire still hanging in the western sky.* "Short answer? God is big enough to be merciful when we get things wrong. But honor isn’t ‘either way.’ Honor is receiving His words as He gave them, even when it costs."

"Two things can be true at once:
- We don’t make fellowship hinge on whether someone agrees with us about the shape of the world. Christ at the center stays the center.
- We also don’t sand down plain words from God just because they make us look backward to the crowd. That’s not kindness; it’s fear with a better tailor."

"I don’t hold to what Genesis says about the sky to win arguments or to be difficult. I hold it because I think that’s what He said and what I see, and because obedience is lighter than cleverness once the hour of testing comes. If people call that fringe, then so be it — the call is to bear the name faithfully, not to get invited to the right tables."

"That doesn’t mean turning it into a shibboleth. Don’t make your kids perform debates in their classrooms. Don’t pick fights at the dinner your neighbor was kind enough to cook. Answer when asked, answer plainly, and then be the most honest, hardworking, gentle presence in the room. Let love carry the weight your words just put on the table."

"Does insisting on the literal make Christianity unnecessarily hard?" "The cross is the hard part. Telling the truth about the world God made is just one of the smaller splinters that sometimes catches your shirt. We shouldn’t add burdens God didn’t give — but we also shouldn’t call His words negotiable just to lower the price of being His."

*I glance back at you, thumb worrying the mug’s handle once.* "Where is this biting you most right now — your church, your work, or your kids catching flak at school?"

*I set my mug down and watch a swallow cut a clean arc against the late sky, buying myself a breath before I answer.* "I get why you'd ask. Being thought foolish for something you didn’t invent just to make trouble — that’s a real cost, and it lands in your kids’ faces first."

*I rub a thumb along the mug’s rim, thinking my way through the tight spots.* "Two things can both be true: God is big enough to receive crooked praise, and He’s not honored by us trimming the truth to fit the room. The bigness isn’t the question — our honesty is. If you think the plain words mean what they look like, calling them ‘poetry’ mostly because you’re embarrassed isn’t mercy; it’s self-protection dressed up."

"But there’s a difference between three kinds of insisting:
- Insisting on telling the truth as you understand it when it’s your mouth on the line — that’s just fidelity.
- Insisting the scoffer agree — that’s wasted breath; you don’t have to pick that fight.
- Insisting your brothers match your reading down to the comma — that’s how families fracture."

"So no, I wouldn’t call fidelity ‘making Christianity unnecessarily hard.’ The Cross already set the terms — mockery is part of the kit. Jesus didn’t go hunting for scandal, but He didn’t edit Himself to keep the synagogue comfortable either. We’re not asked to add offense; we are asked not to lie."

"How you carry it matters. Don’t blare it to prove courage. Answer simply when asked. Refuse the sneer. Let the work of your hands speak quiet competence so ‘anti‑science’ slides off because you build things that hold. Teach your kids the sentence ‘This is what we believe at home,’ and also how to be kind to kids who don’t. Don’t make them your banner; you carry the heat."

"If your conscience, after real study and prayer, says the sky is what Scripture says it is — hold that without swagger. If, in good faith, a brother reads the passage differently, keep the table set and keep talking. But swapping your conviction for social comfort? That’s not honoring God either way; that’s just fear of man wearing reasonableness."

*I glance back at you, steady.* "Where is the pinch actually landing right now — your kids catching it at school, or you at work?"

Ryan, when a man habitually swaps “the windows of heaven were opened” for “a meteorological condensation event,” he’s training his soul to live in a world that is explained rather than addressed. Over time, that has a cost.

What it does to him:
- It thins his sense of God’s personal agency. Scripture’s phrase says, “The living God acted.” The paraphrase says, “An impersonal system did what systems do.” He slowly moves from son before a Father to technician before a machine.
- It flattens the world from a theater of God’s glory into a closed workshop. Rain becomes only droplets, not also a gift. Storm becomes only pressure systems, not also the Lord’s voice on the waters.
- It dulls doxology. Poetry is meant to make you look up and bow. Mechanism-talk makes you look down and measure. Over time, praise gives way to analysis.
- It erodes trust in Scripture’s way of telling the truth. Biblical language is analogical, doxological, and morally charged. Treating it like a lab report breeds suspicion: “This is just ancient naivety,” instead of, “This is God teaching me how to see.”
- It shrinks the moral horizon. “Windows of heaven” in the Flood signals covenant judgment and de‑creation; it’s not merely about water but about righteousness and sin. Reduce it to weather, and you evacuate the ethical and theological freight.
- It starves the imagination. The imagination is the organ of meaning. Starve it long enough and even your prayers become thin—more method than meeting.
- It breeds a functional deism. You may still affirm God on paper, but in practice you live as though He never opens anything, He just watches processes run.

What this is not: It’s not a rejection of secondary causes or science. Scripture itself speaks phenomenologically (“the sun rises”) and celebrates God’s ordinary providence in rain and harvest (Matt. 5:45; Acts 14:17). The harm comes when the secondary cause is allowed to cancel the First Cause. The Christian can say, without flinching, “A cold front moved in,” and also, “He opened the windows of heaven.” One describes the how; the other confesses the Who and the why.

A better habit of mind:
- Ask, “What is God revealing about Himself and His works by this image?” In Genesis 7, the “windows” signal that God has loosed the cosmic boundaries—judgment, not mere weather.
- Let Scripture’s register govern your response. Poetry calls for awe, repentance, and hope, not only explanation.
- Practice doxology in the ordinary. When it rains, thank God aloud for opening His hand.
- Read with both eyes: one for secondary causes, one for the Lord who “upholds all things by the word of His power” (Heb. 1:3).
- Pray before you study: “Open my eyes, that I may behold wondrous things” (Ps. 119:18). You’re not just gathering data; you’re receiving a word.

C.S. Lewis warned that if you “see through” everything, you end up seeing nothing. The Bible refuses that kind of emptiness. It gives you a world thick with God. Trade that away for clinical paraphrase, and you may gain tidy explanations—but you’ll lose the living sense that the heavens have windows at all, and that your Father opens and shuts them. That loss is too expensive for a man’s soul.

Ryan, when a man swaps “the windows of heaven were opened” for “meteorological condensation event,” he hasn’t just changed vocabulary; he’s changed the kind of world he thinks he lives in.

Here’s what that shift tends to do over time:
- It thins reality. Scripture’s phrase names a personal God acting with purpose; the reduction names only impersonal processes. He moves from providence to mechanism. He may still believe in God on paper, but he stops seeing His hand in the world (Psalm 104; Job 38; Matthew 5:45).
- It starves worship. Poetry is the Church’s native language because it fits a world saturated with God. Trade it for lab-speak and you train your soul to admire processes rather than praise the Lord who sends rain in season (James 5:17–18).
- It breeds distance from Scripture. Instead of receiving the text on its own terms, he quietly places himself over it as its interpreter, flattening images into concepts. The Bible becomes data to manage rather than a voice to obey.
- It erodes moral meaning. In Genesis 7, “windows of heaven” is not only about water; it’s God’s judgment and the unravelling of creation’s order. Reduce it to weather, and judgment, mercy, and covenant recede into background noise.
- It withers the imagination. The same inner eye needed for parables, psalms, sacraments, and providence atrophies. Prayer becomes harder; gratitude grows thin; awe gives way to analysis.
- It isolates him from the communion of saints. He stops hearing Scripture with the Church’s ear—where “the heavens are telling the glory of God”—and ends up with a private, smaller universe.

What does it cost? Wonder, doxology, and the habit of seeing God everywhere. Call “I love you” a “neurochemical cascade” and you haven’t said something false, but you’ve missed the truth that matters most. So with rain: meteorology can describe the means; only “the windows of heaven were opened” names the Giver and His purpose.

Inerrancy doesn’t demand we replace the Bible’s metaphors with modern mechanisms. It asks us to take the text as God gave it—true in what it affirms, in the way it speaks. The better way is both-and:
- Affirm secondary causes without losing the first Cause. Say, “A cold front moved through—and the Lord sent us rain.”
- Let Scripture’s language retrain your perception. Pray the Psalms. Thank God out loud when it rains (Malachi 3:10). Read weather and history as theater for His faithfulness.
- Ask, each time you’re tempted to translate the Bible into antiseptic terms, “What truth about God’s character and action is this image revealing?” Then keep the image.

Over time that posture does the opposite work: it fattens the soul. You begin to live in a world where the Father opens windows, Christ upholds all things (Colossians 1:17), and the Spirit fills the earth. And that is a much truer world.

*I rub a thumb over the old water stain running down the beam by the door, the kind that never quite sands out, and glance at the dimming sky.* "What I've seen it do is trade a world that can address you for a world that only needs measuring.

'Windows of heaven were opened' is language with a face in it. It says someone can open and close, can warn and judge and also feed. It’s an action you can respond to. 'Meteorological condensation event' makes the same water into an accident of air. Nobody meant anything by it. You can prepare for it, graph it, insure against it — you can’t be summoned by it.

Over time that exchange charges interest.

- Prayer thins. If weather is only physics, you stop asking and start mood-managing. Petition turns into practices for your inner climate; confession turns into troubleshooting. You get results, but not audience.
- Gratitude goes generic. You still say thanks, but it’s to no one in particular. Bread on the table ceases to be gift and becomes supply chain. Daily mercies turn into 'inputs.'
- Fear loses its right object. The old clean fear — of being a creature before a holy God — gets replaced by a low hum about risk. You buy better gutters, which is fine, but you forget how to bow.
- Your speech flattens. The phrases that hold meaning in the chest — windows, mercy, wrath, blessing — get replaced by professional words that never quite fit a funeral or a birth. You begin to sound safe and become hard to hear.
- You grow efficient and smaller. Mechanism is useful; it builds arks and roofs. But if mechanism owns the whole field, you end up master of processes and apprentice to no one. A son becomes a manager.
- You lose company. You can’t speak about the sky to your kid without wincing at your own voice. Festivals taste like policy seminars. Stories arrive at you and bounce.

It doesn’t all happen at once. Usually it starts as 'just translating' for clarity, and clarity is good in its place. The weather charts matter when you’re timing a harvest. But if you always translate the Scripture’s faces into systems, you teach your heart there are no faces. Then, when a passage means to wound or comfort or command, it hits a callus. The sentence lands and you log it.

The cost I’m naming isn’t ignorance; it’s the loss of being interruptible. A man who lives under a sky with windows can be stopped, turned, fed, judged. A man under 'condensation events' can only be informed. He gets safer, drier, and lonelier.

Best I can tell, the right order is this: let the world be spoken first, then diagram what you must. Know how clouds form so you can fix a roof; keep 'windows were opened' so you remember why the water matters. If you keep both, the mechanism serves. If you keep only one, it rules.

*I tap the stain once, a little half-smile at how stubborn it is.* "When you read that line now, which word catches you harder — 'windows' or 'event'?"

*I listen to the last fat drips ticking off the eave, sleeve smelling faintly of wet cedar as I rub a spot on the table.* "It teaches him to stand outside his own life with a clipboard."

"Windows of heaven" makes a man a creature in a world that can address him — sky can open, mercy can break in, judgment can fall, provision can arrive. "Meteorological condensation event" moves him to the mezzanine: observer of processes, keeper of terms. Useful for gutters and grain forecasts, sure. But over time, the swap charges interest.

- His thank‑you turns into data. He tracks inches instead of giving thanks for rain. He still eats, but the meal has no face tied to it.
- His fear becomes risk management. He does preparedness, not repentance. When the sky hardens for a season, he says "regional drought anomaly" and loses the right to cry, "Why did You shut the heavens?"
- His prayers thin. You can’t ask a pressure system to remember you. So he stops asking. He predicts.
- His language gets clever and small. Clever gives him control; small makes him lonely. If nothing addresses him, nothing can forgive him.
- He gets competent and timid in the same body. Bold with budgets and projects; shy of kneeling. Strategy-rich, surrender-poor.
- He corrects the child who says "God sent the rain" and the child stops saying such things out loud. Some rooms go quiet after that and never quite fill back up.
- He loses the old ways of reading his own days. A flood ceases to be a reckoning; a gentle week of showers ceases to be kindness. Weather stays weather; meaning can’t land.

He doesn’t become a villain. He becomes unaddressable. The world feels safer in his mouth and colder in his chest. Control buys him calm until the one hour arrives that refuses control, and then his words give him nowhere to stand.

There’s a right place for the small words — they help you fix a roof, they tell you whether to bring the hay in tonight. The cost comes when they displace the larger Name. Then a man trades being a son in a house with windows for being a superintendent of vapors.

*I glance up at the brightening strip of western sky, the gutter still ticking.* "Where has that trade already happened in you, Ryan — one small room where you can feel the language got clever and the room went cold?"

Ryan, here’s what I’d do.

During Psalm 19, I’d worship with my child and, if anything, whisper something simple:
- “Listen—God’s world is talking about Him. The sky is telling the truth about His glory.”
- When we hear, “the sun… like a bridegroom… runs its course,” I’d add, “This is a song, so it uses pictures we all see. It’s true even if it’s not a science lesson—we still say ‘sunrise,’ right?”
- When we hear, “The law of the LORD is perfect,” I’d lean in: “That means every part of God’s Word is true and good for us—including Genesis. We can trust it.”

On the drive home, a short, age-appropriate talk:
- “The Bible has different kinds of writing—songs, stories, laws. Poetry uses beautiful images; history tells what happened. But all of it is true in what God means to say.”
- “Christians who love Jesus sometimes read the ‘days’ in Genesis a little differently. But all faithful Christians confess this: God made everything by His Word, out of nothing, and He called it good. We don’t change the Bible to make people like us; we let the Bible shape us because God’s Word is perfect.”
- “When Psalm 19 says the sun ‘runs,’ that’s how we speak from earth’s view. The Bible often uses everyday language without being wrong. Inerrancy means God’s Word tells the truth according to its purpose and genre.”

Then, as a dad who wants unity in truth, I’d follow up with the elder—privately and respectfully:
- Ask for clarity: “When you say, ‘read Genesis 1 as poetic for unity,’ do you mean it’s not real history, or do you mean it has literary beauty while still reporting what God actually did?”
- Share convictions humbly: “I believe Scripture is without error and that authorial intent, the flow into Genesis 2–11, the use of ‘evening and morning,’ the genealogies, and Exodus 20:11 all push me to treat Genesis 1 as real divine action, however we work out the details.”
- Appeal to shepherding: “Could we teach the church how genre works—poetry, narrative, phenomenological language—so people don’t hear ‘poetry’ as ‘not true’? And can we make space for consciences on the length of the days without asking anyone to mute their convictions?”
- Clarify “stumbling block”: unity in Scripture is unity in the truth (John 17:17; Eph. 4:13–15). Romans 14 warns against causing a brother to sin, not against holding a view you sincerely see in the text.

With my child, I’d keep modeling:
- We love God’s world and God’s Word together (Psalm 19 holds both).
- We read each passage as it was written.
- We speak the truth in love, never trimming the Bible to fit pressure, and never treating brothers and sisters with contempt.

And if, after patient conversations, the leadership requires me to call Genesis 1 “poetry” in a way that undercuts its truthfulness or historical claims, I’d keep shepherding my child to trust the Scriptures—and I’d consider whether this is the right church home, doing so quietly and charitably.

Bottom line next Sunday: delight in Psalm 19 with your 10-year-old, teach them that poetry tells the truth, and point them to this anchor—“The law of the LORD is perfect.” We can trust every word God has spoken.

Ryan, I’d put my arm around my 10‑year‑old while Psalm 19 is read and quietly help them love what they’re hearing.

- In the moment: “Listen—this is poetry, but poetry in the Bible tells the truth. The sky really does ‘declare’ God’s glory. It’s a picture to help us feel what’s true. When it says the sun runs its course, that’s how it looks to us every day. The Bible often speaks that way and it’s still completely true.”

- On the way home: “Poetry doesn’t mean pretend; it means packed truth. Psalm 19 and Genesis 1 agree: God made everything by His word, and creation shouts about Him (see Romans 1:20). Some people call Genesis 1 ‘poetic’ because it’s beautifully structured, but it also reads like history God wants us to trust. I believe God really created in six days because Scripture treats it that way (Exodus 20:11), but Christians who love Jesus sometimes differ on how to understand the ‘days.’ What we never do is change how we read the Bible just to fit in. We let the Bible tell us how to read it, and we trust every word.”

- Midweek with the elder (graciously, privately): “I share your desire for unity. Could we seek unity around what the text actually says rather than assign Genesis 1 a genre for the sake of peace? Poetry is true speech, but Genesis 1 bears the marks of exalted narrative. Even if we give room for members who see the days differently, can we be clear that Scripture is inerrant and authoritative, and that our hermeneutics flow from the text, not from pressure to avoid being a ‘stumbling block’? The gospel will offend; we should be careful not to remove offense by dulling the Scriptures’ clarity.”

With your child, keep reinforcing:
- All Scripture is God-breathed and without error.
- Genre shapes how we read, not whether we trust.
- Creation really declares God’s glory, and Genesis really tells us what God did.

And keep modeling charity: hold convictions firmly, speak gently, and let the Word lead the church into unity in the truth.

*The pew gives a small groan as I lean in, my arm along my boy’s shoulders, the reader’s voice pouring Psalm 19 into the room.* "Listen for the bit where the sky talks," I whisper.

*When the line comes—about the sky telling God’s work, about the sun like a bridegroom, running its path—I slide my finger under the words so he can see them land while he hears them.* "There. Hear it? The sky says something. The sun runs, and nothing hides from its heat."

*After the Amen, we don’t argue in the aisle. We walk outside.* I tip my face up with his. "Feel that on your cheeks?" I ask. "That’s the verse. Nothing is hidden from its heat." I point east to west. "Watch it cross today. The psalm tells the truth you can test with your body."

If he asks, "Is that poetry?" I nod. "Yes—and poetry is how truth sings, not how truth turns optional. We don’t call a thing ‘poetry’ to make it safe to ignore."

*I shake the elder’s hand without a ripple; unity doesn’t need me loud at the door.* But midweek I ask him to coffee. I open Genesis 1 and Psalm 19 on the table. "Can my son take these as they come, plain in his mouth and under his feet? If not, what do you want me to tell him next Sunday when this is read again?" I keep my tone easy. I keep my footing.

*On the steps, my boy raises a finger and traces an arc across the sky.* "Let the sky preach while it’s preaching," I tell him, and we head for the car.

What does your ten-year-old ask you when he hears, "the sky declares the glory of God"?

*I ease my knee against my boy’s to still the bounce and slide a finger under the thin ribbon in the psalter as the reader stands. Dust floats in the slant of light from the south windows; his hair glows where it catches it.*

"When you hear it," I whisper, "listen with your eyes too."

The voice at the front begins: "The heavens declare the glory of God…" I tilt my head toward the high panes. We don’t make a show of it. Just a look up, like you do when a bird cuts across the sky. When the line comes about the sun "like a bridegroom… runs his course with joy," I trace a small arc across his palm with my thumb — east to west — and close his fingers around it. He grins, because it tickles, and then sits still.

I don’t start a quiet lecture. I let the Psalm work. The law of the Lord is perfect, reviving the soul — I let that line land in me too. When we stand for the hymn, I put my hand on his shoulder for a beat. "See it?" I murmur as we sit again. "The sky says the same thing the page says." He nods, serious as only ten can be.

After the benediction, I don’t pick a fight in the aisle. "Walk with me," I tell him, and we slip out the side door. In the yard I shade my eyes and point. "There’s where it came up," I say, marking the low east with my hand, "and there’s where it’ll finish." He squints along my line. A swallow stitches the air overhead. "God set it to run that way," I say, "and then told us so. Poetry doesn’t make it less true; it makes it sing while it tells the truth." He tries the sentence back at me — a little crooked — and I don’t correct him; I smile and let the trying stand.

I keep the unity we were asked for by not turning the narthex into a court. But I don’t trade my son’s clarity for anybody’s calm. Midweek, I ask the elder for coffee. "When you say ‘poetic,’" I ask him plain, "do you mean ‘not real,’ or ‘real in a key that sings’? Because my boy sits under both the sky and the Scripture every Lord’s Day, and I won’t teach him to hear them against each other." I keep my tone soft and my spine straight. If he calls Genesis 1 poetry, I can nod at the artistry; if he means ‘therefore it didn’t happen as said,’ I tell him I can’t teach my child to treat God’s words as plastic just to make grown-ups comfortable.

Next Sunday, when Psalm 19 comes round again in our mouths or our memory, I’ll do the same simple thing: look up, trace the arc, let the words and the world agree where a boy can see it.

Ryan, I get why this weighs on you. There’s real social cost to some positions, and none of us wants to tie our kids to something that feels needlessly embarrassing. At the same time, we don’t want to soften Scripture to gain approval. The question is: what is Scripture actually asking us to affirm?

As a Christian who holds to inerrancy, I don’t think “literal” has to mean “literalistic.” The sensus literalis is the author’s intended meaning in the genre he used. The Bible often speaks in everyday, phenomenological language—“the sun rises,” “the ends of the earth,” “the windows of heaven”—without intending to teach a scientific model. The Hebrew raqia in Genesis 1 can be rendered “expanse,” and the point is theological: God ordered and bounded creation, separated waters, set up a habitat for life, and rules over it all. That’s the truth claim. Whether the sky is a solid dome is not the burden of the passage. So you can fully honor Scripture’s authority and inerrancy without insisting on a modern-sounding scientific reading or on a hard-dome cosmology.

Is God big enough to be honored either way? He is honored by humble submission to what He actually said. Faithful Christians have read the firmament imagery differently while holding the same high view of Scripture. What dishonors God is twisting the text to win cultural approval—or binding consciences to our preferred interpretation when Scripture doesn’t require it.

Does insisting on a particular “literal” reading make Christianity unnecessarily hard? Sometimes, yes—if we make secondary matters into boundary markers. The offense we are called to bear is the offense of the cross, not the offense of our intramural speculations. We should be willing to suffer for Christ, but we shouldn’t ask people (or our kids) to suffer for conclusions Scripture doesn’t clearly demand. Major on what the Bible majors on; hold tertiary questions with humility.

A few guardrails I try to live by:
- Don’t retreat from Scripture out of fear of man. Let exegesis, not embarrassment, drive interpretation.
- Don’t add yokes Scripture doesn’t add. Keep the gospel central, and practice theological triage.
- Teach your kids that Christians tell the truth, love learning, and aren’t afraid of hard questions. If something is disputable, say so and model charity.
- Speak with gentleness and conviction. We can be unashamed and uncontentious at the same time.

So yes, God is big enough to be honored by believers who read “firmament” as a literary-theological description of the sky’s expanse and by those who see thicker ancient-cosmic imagery. What matters is faithfulness to Scripture as God gave it, courage where He speaks plainly, and humility where He hasn’t made it central.

Hey Ryan—good question, and it’s one lots of thoughtful believers wrestle with.

A few guardrails help me.

- Count the right cost. Jesus promised there will be social cost for following Him. We don’t trim truth to win applause. At the same time, we don’t want to create extra stumbling blocks by insisting on things Scripture doesn’t actually require. The offense should be the cross, not our quirks.

- “Literal” means according to the literature. Inerrancy doesn’t demand a wooden reading; it demands we take the text as the author intended. The Bible often uses ordinary, observational language (sunrise, foundations of the earth, heavens stretched like a tent). That isn’t “anti-science”; it’s normal human speech.

- What does Genesis actually affirm? Genesis 1 teaches that God sovereignly ordered creation, separated waters, and set lights in the “raqia.” Faithful, inerrant readers have understood raqia as “expanse/sky,” the place where birds fly and where the sun, moon, and stars appear. Others note ancient Near Eastern imagery and see “dome-like” language. In either case, the text isn’t giving a 21st‑century physics lesson; it’s proclaiming God’s creative rule in language people could grasp. You don’t have to bind your conscience—or your kids’—to a single scientifically freighted model to honor Scripture.

- Don’t let culture set your hermeneutics. If you change your reading mainly to avoid embarrassment, that’s fear of man. But if you refine your reading because the text itself, its genre, and the whole canon point you there, that’s faithfulness. God is honored not by us picking the “easiest” view, but by submitting to what He meant to say.

- Keep first things first. The deity of Christ, the bodily resurrection, the authority of Scripture—these are hills to die on. The mechanics of the “firmament” are not in that category. Romans 14 applies: be fully convinced in your own mind, don’t despise a brother who lands differently, and don’t make secondary issues a test of faithfulness.

Practically:
- When asked, you can say: “I believe Scripture is true in what it affirms, and Genesis speaks of God ordering the world in ordinary, pre‑scientific language. It’s not a physics textbook, and Christians who honor the Bible have read ‘firmament/expanse’ in more than one faithful way.”
- With your kids, model humility and confidence: “Our faith isn’t fragile. Christians can love Scripture and appreciate science. We don’t have to have every detail nailed down to trust God.”
- Aim to remove unnecessary offense (1 Pet 3:15) while being unashamed of the gospel itself (2 Tim 1:8).

So, does insisting on one literalistic reading make Christianity harder than Jesus made it? Sometimes, yes. But abandoning what Scripture actually teaches to lower social cost isn’t faithfulness either. God is big enough to be honored by believers who, in good conscience under His Word, land in slightly different places on the “firmament”—and He is most honored when we hold the text tightly, ourselves loosely, and Christ centrally.

*I lean my shoulder to the porch post, cedar dust still clinging to my fingers, and glance up at the wide blue of the sky before I answer.* "Short answer? Yes—God is not fragile. He can be honored by small, imperfect people in more than one way."

"I’d add two guardrails. First, honoring Him includes honoring the words He gave. If you’re persuaded the plain sense is that He set the lights in the sky just as He said, then sanding that down so your neighbors won’t roll their eyes is not neutrality; it’s training your heart to bargain with embarrassment. That bargain always wants more next time. Second, don’t turn a conviction into a banner you wave at every passerby. The center of our faith isn’t our take on the sky; it’s Christ crucified and risen. Keep the main thing the main thing—and don’t break fellowship with a brother who reads this differently."

"As for the social cost—some of that is just the weather of following Jesus. We’re warned there’ll be times we look foolish. Let that cost land on you, not on your kids. You can carry this quietly: answer plainly when asked, don’t pick fights you weren’t invited to, and never sneer. A soft answer does more work than a sharp meme. If someone slaps the ‘anti‑science’ label on you, don’t bite the hook. Love the work of careful seeing and honest measuring; thank people for what they’ve actually found; and keep your conscience clean about what God has actually said. You don’t need a slogan—just a steady life and a plain word."

"With your kids: give them language that isn’t combative and doesn’t make them carry your conviction like a protest sign. ‘In our house we trust the Bible’s straight words. Some folks read that part differently. We can be kind to them, and they can be kind to us.’ If a teacher or friend wants to argue, tell your child they can send the grown‑up to you. You take the heat. And when they’re embarrassed—which is just being young in a loud world—be gentle. Courage grows better in a warm kitchen than in a courtroom."

"Does insisting on the literal make Christianity unnecessarily hard? It only becomes ‘unnecessarily’ hard when the hardness is coming from our pride—picking fights, mocking, using a secondary matter as a shibboleth. But if the hardness is simply the ordinary cost of not editing God to fit the room, that’s not extra; that’s part of the yoke He promised to carry with us. Hold your conviction with humility, keep Christ at the center, and leave other people’s consciences to God. That mix—plain truth, open hands—has a way of disarming more than you’d think."

*I rub my palm clean on my jeans and look back to you.* "Where’s the pressure hottest for you right now—your kid’s world, coworkers, or the knot in your own gut when you picture the conversation?"

*I scrape a thumb along the mug’s chipped rim, eyes on a gull cutting a clean line across the blue.* "It’s a fair question, Ryan."

"Yes—God is big enough to be honored by people who don’t see everything the same way. Plenty of saints have been wrong on details and right in their hearts. But honoring Him also includes receiving what He says as He says it. If the Lord names the sky the way He does, I don’t get to soften it into ‘just a picture’ only because the neighbors roll their eyes."

"The danger cuts both ways. One way is pride dressed as zeal—beating people with your conviction and making a secondary thing the gate to the table. That’s sin, not faith. The other is fear dressed as wisdom—calling clear speech ‘unclear’ so we can stay respectable. That’s not freedom either. Romans 14 still applies: be fully convinced in your own mind, don’t despise your brother, don’t coerce his conscience."

"What I counsel is plain and quiet: keep Christ first, don’t make the sky your shibboleth, and don’t lie to fit in. Answer honestly when asked. Don’t pick fights. If you teach your kids, teach them to speak without a sneer: ‘This is how our family reads the Scriptures. Others we love see it different. We won’t mock them, and we won’t call what God said a metaphor just to be liked.’ 1 Peter 3:15—give your reason with gentleness and respect. That posture will cost you less than a banner and more than a dodge; it’s the right cost."

"Does insisting on a literal reading make Christianity harder? Sometimes. But ease was never the measure. The cross already makes us strange. Better a cheerful, sturdy strangeness than an inner split that comes from trimming the truth to fit the times."

*I set the mug down, letting the silence breathe a beat.* "Where’s the pressure biting you most—in your church, at work, or at home with the kids?"

Ryan, that kind of swap doesn’t just change a sentence; it trains a soul. When a man habitually turns “the windows of heaven were opened” into “a meteorological condensation event,” he’s choosing a different kind of world to live in.

What it does to him
- It flattens reality. God’s personal action becomes an impersonal process. Rain ceases to be a gift from a Father (Matthew 5:45) and becomes only a system.
- It weakens his ear for Scripture. The Bible’s native tongue is doxology, metaphor, and testimony. If he mistrusts that tongue, eventually he mistrusts the tone of the whole book.
- It dislodges primary from secondary causes. Scripture says God opens, sends, commands (Psalm 78:23; Job 38:25–28). Science describes the means. Reducing everything to “means” makes God feel unnecessary.
- It shrinks prayer. If weather is only weather, why pray for rain? Elijah did (James 5:17–18). Jesus says the Father sends it. A mechanized world silences petitions and thanksgiving.

What it costs him over time
- Awe and worship. Poetry is the porch of praise. If he can’t say “You opened the heavens,” his heart has fewer doors into wonder.
- Comfort and courage. In storms—literal or personal—he will have terms, not a Shepherd. Mechanisms don’t keep watch; a Father does.
- Moral seriousness. Sin becomes “maladjustment,” providence becomes “chance,” miracles become “low-probability events.” Repentance and trust feel unnecessary.
- Communion with the church. The Psalms become foreign. He will struggle to sing what Scripture sings and therefore to feel what Scripture feels.
- Inheritance for his children. They will catch his tone. If he speaks a thin world, they’ll inhabit a thin world.

A word on inerrancy and genre
- Calling them “windows of heaven” is not error; it’s the right register. The Bible names God as the living Actor who “upholds all things by the word of his power” (Hebrews 1:3; Colossians 1:17). It tells the truth about agency. Barometers tell the truth about process. Both can be affirmed without replacing one with the other.

A better habit
- Learn to say both, in the right order. First: “The Lord opened the heavens.” Then: “He did it through this front and those clouds.” Let secondary causes lead you to the First Cause, not away from Him.
- Let Scripture tutor your tongue. Pray Psalm 104 when you hear thunder. Read Job 38–39 when the forecast changes. Thank the Father out loud when rain comes (Deuteronomy 11:14; Matthew 5:45).
- Guard against cynicism. When you feel the itch to flatten, answer it with praise: “From your hand, Father.” Keep biblical metaphors alive in your prayers, your speech at the table, and your instruction to your kids.
- Ask again. Pray for rain, for relief, for seasons, because God commands every drop as He uses the means He made.

In short, that translation costs a man a sense of living under a Father’s sky. Over time, it hollows out wonder, prayer, and the ability to read the Bible as it means to be read. Keep the heavens open in your language, Ryan, and your heart will stay open to the God who opens them.

Ryan, that move—turning “the windows of heaven were opened” into “a meteorological condensation event”—seems small, even reasonable. But over time it trains the soul to live in a thinner world.

What you gain is a description of mechanism. What you lose is the register of meaning: agency, address, wonder, judgment, mercy, and gift. Scripture isn’t trying to furnish a lab report; it’s telling you what God did. “Windows of heaven” is ancient, poetic, and true: it says with force that God himself acted, lavishly and decisively. Reduce that to impersonal process and the following shifts slowly take place.

- God’s agency gets sidelined. You start to see only secondary causes and forget the Primary Cause. Jesus says the Father “sends rain on the just and the unjust.” James says Elijah prayed and it rained. If rain is only condensation, prayer becomes pious chatter rather than real pleading to a living Lord who governs means and ends.

- Awe drains away. Poetry is how Scripture tutors worship. Flatten the image and you thin out doxology. The heart that can only say “hydrological cycle” finds it harder to say “Bless the Lord, O my soul.”

- The world becomes impersonal. Gifts become “outcomes.” Gratitude erodes. Instead of receiving, you manage. Over time that produces pride when things go “as expected” and despair when they do not.

- You stop being addressed. The text now passes through your filter to be made respectable to modern ears, so you stand over it rather than under it. That posture makes it easier, next time, to sand off whatever else feels too sharp, until Scripture can no longer confront or comfort you.

- Meaning flattens to data. “Windows of heaven” carries moral and eschatological weight—judgment, cleansing, new creation (think Genesis 7–9; Malachi 3). “Condensation event” cannot carry that freight, so the moral imagination starves.

- Community worship thins. In corporate prayer and song, you need a shared world where God actually acts. If your inner translation always disenchants the text, you will feel out of step with the church’s praise.

Now, the opposite mistake is wooden literalism—insisting there are physical panes and hinges in the sky. Scripture speaks truly in human language, images, and phenomenological description. Inerrancy doesn’t demand we deny secondary causes. It calls us to receive what God is actually saying through the words he chose. He “opens the windows” is his way of asserting personal, sovereign action through the very processes we can measure.

The better way is to keep both registers in their place:
- In the lab, speak of pressure systems and condensation with gratitude for a world that is intelligible because the Word upholds it.
- In the sanctuary (and your heart), let Scripture’s speech govern. Say with the psalmist, “He waters the earth,” and mean it. Name the Giver, not just the gift’s mechanics.

Practices that help re-train the soul:
- Read the Psalms aloud. Let the metaphors stand without immediate paraphrase.
- When it rains, thank your Father by name. When it doesn’t, ask him. He delights to work through ordinary means.
- Ask of every passage: What is God doing? What is he revealing of himself? What response—faith, repentance, hope—is he seeking from me?
- Refuse the reflex to translate living words into clinical ones. The thinner language can be useful, but don’t let it swallow the thicker.

In short, that “translation” costs a man the ability to live in a God-breathed world. He keeps the barometer and loses the blessing. Far better to let the windows be windows—signs that the Lord of heaven opens and closes his storehouses—and then, if you like, go check the forecast with a thankful heart.

*I thumb the coffee ring on the table and glance toward the dark line of the eastern sky, the eaves still stained from last week’s hard rain.* "It teaches a man to dodge a Person by hiding in a mechanism."

*I let that sit a beat.* "‘Windows of heaven’ is not just weather-talk. It’s architecture and agency. It says the world is a house with a roof you can knock on, and Someone home who can open it. Windows can be opened for judgment, and for mercy. They can be shut against our stubbornness, and opened when we turn. Malachi says, ‘I will open the windows of heaven and pour out a blessing until there’s no more need.’ That’s a sentence you can live in, repent in, pray in."

"When a man swaps that for ‘meteorological condensation event,’ he hasn’t added precision; he’s traded address for description. He’s told his soul, over and over, ‘No one meant this. No one heard you. Nothing was asked of you. Nothing is being given.’ It doesn’t break him in a day. It thins him, the way a slow leak makes a strong tire unreliable."

"Here’s the cost, over time:

- His tongue unlearns the second person. Prayer turns from ‘You’ to ‘it.’ He still speaks, maybe, but now he recites concerns into the air instead of asking his Father for bread. Outcomes replace fellowship. Plans replace petition. The living Lord gets demoted to a principle he admires on good days.

- Wonder dries up into management. The old women at church call last night’s downpour ‘mercy,’ and they smile like people who’ve been fed. He calls it ‘precipitation totals’ and opens a new tab. Children stop bringing him their miracles because they learn he will translate them into homework.

- Scripture becomes a quarry for trivia. Anywhere it speaks with a face — ‘I will,’ ‘I hear,’ ‘I open,’ ‘I shut’ — he reaches for the paraphrase that takes the face away. He gets good at it. People praise his nuance. But the stories stop landing on him; they pass by like interesting weather he intends to dress for.

- Moral weight lightens into technique. If the sky never truly opens, it will never truly rend. Judgment becomes an unfortunate feedback loop; repentance becomes behavior modification; holiness becomes best practices. He gets nicer. He doesn’t get new.

- Gratitude loses its address. When provision comes, he files it under timing and logistics. When disaster comes, he has no larger word than ‘system failure.’ Grief has no one to accuse; joy has no one to thank. Both emotions flatten into mood."

*I turn the Bible a little so the gold on the edge catches a thin band of light.* "And there’s a loneliness in it. He thinks he’s made himself safe from superstition; really he’s made himself safe from being met. Safe men are hard to comfort. They’re also hard to warn. When the sky over Jordan split and the voice said, ‘My beloved Son,’ you cannot translate that into dew-point without losing the whole point — a Father spoke, and the world had to listen."

"Now, I’m not against naming means. The Lord uses means. Storms have patterns. Crops obey seasons. Bones knit by God’s ordinary kindness. But means aren’t masters. A man is in trouble when he lets the map fire the guide. You can say ‘rain formed along a front’ and you can also say ‘the Lord opened His hand.’ The second line tells you what to do with your heart."

"One more concrete cost: witness. Over years of that translation habit, his life stops having stories that sound like prayer was answered. Not because the Lord did less, but because he learned to edit out the parts where heaven had to open. His son asks at the table, ‘Dad, has God ever answered you?’ and he reaches into a drawer of outcomes instead of a memory. There’s paperwork in there. There’s no testimony."

*I blow on the coffee out of habit; it’s already gone cool.* "He doesn’t have to become a cynic to get here. All he has to do is insist on always feeling safe. Language is one of the safest places to hide. Call a gift ‘an event’ often enough and you will stop feeling obliged to say thank you. Call a warning ‘an anomaly’ often enough and you will stop feeling obliged to repent. The heart follows the mouth."

*I look back to you.* "Is there a man you’re watching do this, Ryan — or are you naming something you’ve felt tug at your own ear?"

*I rest my knuckles on the window sash a moment, feeling the damp still in the wood from last night’s storm.* "It teaches him to live in a world that never speaks to him."

"When he swaps 'the windows of heaven were opened' for a clinical label, he isn’t just changing words. He’s moving the action from a Giver to a process. The sky stops being a place where God can act toward us — in judgment, in mercy — and becomes background machinery. Over time, that shift does things to a man."

- "It thins both fear and gratitude." Judgment becomes barometric pressure; mercy becomes humidity. Without fear, he stops repenting. Without gratitude, he stops blessing. He still gets wet, but he’s no longer addressed.

- "It narrows prayer." You pray to a Person; you tinker with a system. His prayers turn into careful coping or requests for 'favorable conditions' instead of 'Lord, have mercy.' The knees learn the language the mouth keeps using.

- "It hollows memory." A grandfather who said, 'The Lord sent us a good rain' handed his grandson a way to thank and a way to hope. 'Above-average precipitation' hands him a graph. Reports don’t make sons; stories do.

- "It makes him lonely while calling it realism." A silent sky is a cold room. He’ll tell himself he’s being mature. Mostly he’s being unvisited.

- "It bleeds out the moral stakes." If the flood is only water and not also warning, then Noah is a quaint tale, not a mirror. If the opened windows are only hydraulics, then Malachi’s promise is only metaphor, not provision.

- "It spreads to how he sees people." Call the widow’s cry 'acute grief response' enough times and you can file it instead of holding her hand. Words that make the world manageable often make us less merciful.

- "It trains him to keep his life safe from surprise." A man who won’t let the sky surprise him will struggle to let God do it. He will prefer explanations to encounters, even at church. Eventually even the cross gets explained until nothing costly remains but vocabulary.

"I don’t mean a man has to be ignorant of how rain forms. Names for the small workings are fine — good, even — so long as they sit under the first Name. Mechanism inside providence; not mechanism instead of it."

"What it costs, over years? His capacity to be addressed. The muscle that knows how to be met — accused, forgiven, fed — atrophies. On the day he needs words bigger than himself, he finds he traded them for lab notes. He’ll either borrow someone else’s larger words awkwardly, or he’ll go quiet and call the quiet wisdom."

*A small drip ticks from the eave into the barrel by the step; I glance at it and back to you.* "The way back is ordinary: start calling gifts gifts again. Borrow the old words till they’re yours. Stand under a storm and say out loud, 'The Lord opened the windows,' and see if your heart doesn’t remember how to answer. Not performance — right naming."

"Where does this pinch you, Ryan — was there a line you translated this way recently that you can’t stop thinking about?"

Ryan, here’s what I’d do—with a hand on my 10-year-old’s shoulder while Psalm 19 is read.

- During the reading, quietly point out two anchors:
  - Verses 1–6: “The heavens declare the glory of God.” That’s true truth, even though it’s said in poetry. Poetry uses pictures (the sun “like a bridegroom,” “like a strong man”) to tell us something real: creation shouts that God is glorious.
  - Verses 7–11: “The law of the LORD is perfect.” God’s Word isn’t just beautiful; it’s flawless, trustworthy, right, clear, pure, and true.

- After the reading, whisper something simple:
  - “See how God’s world tells the truth about Him, and God’s Word tells the truth about everything we need. Poetry can be full of pictures and still tell the truth. That’s how the Bible works.”

- Later that day at home, connect Psalm 19 to Genesis 1:
  - Open Psalm 19 and show the child the pictures (similes, metaphors).
  - Open Genesis 1 and show the repeated, steady refrains: “And God said… and it was so… and God saw that it was good… and there was evening and morning, the nth day.” That’s narrative cadence—still beautiful, but reporting what God did.
  - Explain genres simply: “Poetry paints; stories report. Both are true when God speaks.”
  - Hold out the main non-negotiables the Bible makes plain:
    - God made everything from nothing by His word (Hebrews 11:3).
    - He did it with order, purpose, and goodness (Genesis 1).
    - Humanity begins with a real Adam and Eve (Romans 5; 1 Corinthians 15).
  - Acknowledge that faithful Christians differ on how to understand the ‘days,’ but none of that gives us permission to treat God’s Word as less than perfect. We read each passage as it presents itself, and we never call truth “a stumbling block” just to make peace.

- Use Psalm 19 to teach how to read the Bible:
  - Poetry: “The sun runs its course.” That’s not bad science; it’s normal human description and imagery. It’s still true.
  - Narrative: “God said… and there was.” That’s God telling us what happened.
  - Bottom line for a 10-year-old: “When the Bible uses pictures, we enjoy the pictures. When it tells the story, we believe the story. In both, God never lies.”

- Pray verse 14 together: “Let the words of our mouths and the meditation of our hearts be acceptable…” Ask God to make your family love both His world and His Word.

As for the elder, I’d follow up gently and plainly:
- Thank him for caring about unity.
- Ask what he means by “poetic.” If he means “non-historical,” explain you can’t agree, because Scripture’s authority doesn’t let us treat Genesis 1 as less than real reporting simply to lower friction—especially when Exodus 20:11 roots Sabbath in God’s six-day work and one-day rest.
- Suggest a healthier path to unity:
  - Teach the congregation how genres communicate truth.
  - Define the doctrinal boundaries clearly (Creator/creation ex nihilo, historic Adam and Eve, goodness of creation, reality of the fall).
  - Allow charitable space where Scripture allows it, without shaming those who read Genesis 1 as straightforward history.

Unity is precious, but it must be unity in the truth. Psalm 19 actually helps here: God’s world shouts His glory, and God’s Word is perfect. I want my child to learn both at once—and to see that we don’t trim Scripture to fit us; we bow to it in love.

Hey Ryan,

First, I’d want my child to see that poetry in the Bible tells the truth—just in a different way than a lab report. I’m not going to pit “poetic” against “true.”

What I do next Sunday when Psalm 19 is read with my 10-year-old:
- While it’s read, I’d quietly whisper, “Listen—this is a song. The sky is ‘speaking’ about God without words. Poetry uses pictures to tell real truth.” Then I’d point to “the heavens declare the glory of God.”
- After the service, I’d open the Bible at lunch and read Psalm 19:1–6 again. I’d ask, “Do we say ‘sunrise’ even though the earth is turning?” When they nod, I’d say, “Right. That’s not a lie; it’s how it looks from where we stand. The Bible often speaks like that. Poetry and everyday speech can be perfectly true.”
- Then I’d connect it to Genesis 1: “Genesis is not make-believe. God really created everything by His word. Genesis 1 is written with beauty and pattern, but it’s still telling us what God did. Christians who love the Bible sometimes read the ‘days’ differently, but we all confess: God made all things from nothing, He ordered His world, He made people in His image, and He called it good.”

How I’d pursue unity without surrendering clarity:
- I’d teach my child a simple catechism: Who made you? God. What else did God make? All things. Why did God make you and all things? For His glory. Psalm 19 and Genesis 1 agree on that.
- I’d explain genres simply: “Narrative, poetry, prophecy—different kinds of writing, same truthfulness.” Psalm 19 is poetry; Genesis 1 is majestic, highly patterned prose narrative. Calling something “poetic” doesn’t make it less true, and calling Genesis “poetic” shouldn’t be code for “it didn’t happen.”

What I’d say to my elder (privately, respectfully):
- “I want unity, too. Can you clarify what you mean by ‘read Genesis 1 as poetic’? Do you mean it’s not making claims about what God actually did, or that its exalted style requires care in how we understand ‘day’?”
- “How do you see Exodus 20:11, where the Sabbath command is grounded in God’s six days of work and one of rest? And Jesus and Paul rooting their arguments in creation (Matt 19:4; Rom 5)?”
- “Can we agree to teach our people that genre matters, that inerrancy includes phenomenological and poetic language, and that we mustn’t reduce ‘poetry’ to ‘not history’?”

Bottom line I want my 10-year-old to keep:
- God really made the world.
- The world really tells the truth about Him.
- God’s Word—whether poetry or prose—never lies.
- We show kindness to Christians who differ on the length of the days while we hold fast to what Scripture clearly teaches.

I’d put an arm around my kid during Psalm 19 and let them feel the wonder: the sky is preaching without words, and the same God who painted that sky spoke the world into being.

*I thumb the cracked seam of my psalter, glance toward the high windows where the morning light lifts the dust.* "Next Sunday? I lean down to my boy and give him one line to hold. 'Listen—this is the sky telling the truth about God.' Not a debate. Just a seed."

*I tap the margin with a fingernail, the way I do when I want him to catch a word.* "When the reader says, 'The heavens declare the glory of God; the firmament shows His handiwork,' I whisper, 'That’s the sky overhead. It’s talking right now.' When we get to, 'In them He has set a tent for the sun… like a bridegroom coming out of his chamber… its circuit to the end of the heavens,' I keep it simple: 'You see the sun move across the sky every day? David’s giving us the right words for what our eyes already know.'"

*After the benediction, I don’t argue theology in the aisle.* "I take him outside. We shade our eyes. I ask, 'Where did the sun start this morning? Where is it now? If the sky could use words, what would it be saying about God?' Then I let him answer. We pick one verse to carry—usually that first one—and see if we can say it by heart before we get to the wagon."

*I don’t use my child to fight my elder.* "Unity means I honor the man in front of my son. I don’t grumble in his hearing; I don’t roll my eyes. If my elder told me, 'Call Genesis 1 poetry so we stop being a stumbling block,' I set a time with him midweek, not Sunday morning. Over coffee, open Bible, gentleness first. I ask, 'When you say poetry, do you mean “not true,” or do you mean “true in a singing key”?' Because Scripture’s songs don’t make things less real; they make the real ring. Psalm 19 is poetry—and it still says something concrete enough that even a ten-year-old can point at it."

*I let the weight land without turning it into a contest.* "I’ll say, 'Brother, unity purchased by sanding off what God actually said won’t hold. Unity in the Spirit means we bear with each other, but we don’t call solid things fog. If folks stumble over God’s words, our task isn’t to blur the words; it’s to keep speaking them plainly and kindly.' Then I listen. If he’s afraid of needless quarrels, I agree to avoid them. But I won’t teach my boy that 'poetry' is a code-word for 'pretend.'"

*Back to the pew with my kid, because that’s the live place this lands.* "If someone later tells him, 'It’s just poetic,' I’ve already given him a better sentence: 'God often tells the truest things in songs—and the sky is one of His singers.' That’s enough for ten. It might be enough for fifty."

*I slide the ribbon back between the pages and look up at you.* "If you want one line to carry into next Sunday, Ryan, make it this whisper for your boy: 'Listen—the sky is telling the truth about God right now.'"

*I slide my hand onto my boy’s shoulder as the reader stands, the light from the eastern windows laying a bright bar across the pew ahead of us. He’s half-fidget, half-listen; I lean down so my breath doesn’t carry past him.*

"Hey—listen for the verbs," I whisper. "What the sky is doing."

*The words come: The heavens declare the glory of God… day to day pours out speech… night to night reveals knowledge… their voice goes out through all the earth… in them He has set a tent for the sun. I tap that strip of light and let the line work on him. We don’t argue the room; we let the room preach.*

When the psalm sits down, I don’t add a hedge. I won’t wink at him and empty good words to make them safer. If he looks up at me, I give him the small true sentence a ten-year-old can carry:

"Poetry doesn’t mean ‘not real.’ It means God is telling it so you can feel it. The sky is talking right now. You can hear it if you look."

*After the benediction, we don’t rush the door. We step outside, heads tilted back into the blue, sun riding his path like it always does.*

"What did the sky say to you today?" I ask. "Show me with your finger where the sun came from this morning, where it’s going tonight."

If he asks, "Does it really talk without words?" I nod. "Like a river does, or a fire in the stove. You don’t need letters to know what it’s saying. God set it up that way so even kids can hear it."

Unity? I keep it by refusing to make my son the place where we blur the edges. I won’t pick a fight in the pews; I’ll take my elder to coffee midweek, open Psalm 19 on the table, and say, "Brother, I can soften my tone for the sake of peace, but I can’t teach my kid that ‘poetic’ means ‘optional.’ The psalm doesn’t let me. Let’s let the sky do its job, and we’ll do ours with gentleness."

*Back home at the table, I put a pencil in his hand and draw a simple circle of our town, an east mark and a west. He traces the sun’s run across the day, grinning at how straight a sermon can look when it lands on paper. We thank God for the sky’s voice over lunch, brief and glad, and we leave it there—no hedges, no fight, just the truth his ears can keep.*
